oneLineSummary: A Python variable is a name that points to an object in memory — the type lives on the object, not the name.
summary:
Python variables are **name-to-object bindings** stored in namespace dictionaries. Unlike statically-typed languages where a variable is a typed memory box, Python variables are labels that can be moved to point at any object. The type belongs to the object. This is **dynamic typing**.

The key mechanical fact: `=` never copies an object. It copies a reference (memory address). For mutable objects (lists, dicts), this means multiple names can silently share the same object.
preInterviewChecklist: Can I explain what `=` does in one sentence WITHOUT saying 'equals'?
Can I name 5 invalid variable name patterns and why each fails?
Can I explain the difference between mutable and immutable and give an example of each?
Can I predict what `a = [1,2]; b = a; b.append(3); print(a)` outputs and WHY?
Can I explain the LEGB rule using my own example?
Can I explain what `global` and `nonlocal` do and when each is needed?
Can I explain why `list = []` is dangerous?
Can I write the fix for the late-binding closure bug?
Can I explain what `is None` tests vs `== None`?

memoryTricks.trick: Think of = as an arrow pointing LEFT: `score ← 95`. Whatever is on the right goes INTO the name on the left. Never right-to-left.
**'Last Exits, Goes Broad'** — Local (current function), Enclosing (outer functions), Global (module), Built-in (Python itself). Python always starts at the most local scope and expands outward.

**MIL** — Mutable Is Listlike (list, dict, set). Everything else (int, str, float, bool, tuple) is immutable. When you change an immutable, Python creates a NEW object. When you change a mutable, Python modifies the EXISTING object.
Assignment of a list is like giving someone the KEY to your house — not a copy of the house. They can come in and rearrange the furniture. Use `.copy()` to give them a copy of the house.
Always use **`is None`** — remember: 'None IS none' (identity). Never `== None` (equality can be overridden by custom classes).

keyTakeaways.point: Variables are name → object bindings, not typed boxes.
keyTakeaways.why: This explains why the same name can hold different types and why type() returns the object's type, not the name's type.

keyTakeaways.point: `=` copies a reference — never the object itself.
keyTakeaways.why: Critical for understanding why mutations through one name affect all other names pointing to the same mutable object.

keyTakeaways.point: Mutable objects (list, dict, set) are shared by reference; immutable objects (int, str, tuple) appear to be independent because 'changing' them creates new objects.
keyTakeaways.why: This is the source of the most common Python bugs for developers coming from other languages.

keyTakeaways.point: Python resolves names using LEGB: Local → Enclosing → Global → Built-in.
keyTakeaways.why: Explains NameError, UnboundLocalError, and why `global` and `nonlocal` exist.

keyTakeaways.point: Assignment inside a function makes the name local for the ENTIRE function — even lines before the assignment.
keyTakeaways.why: The cause of UnboundLocalError — Python scans the whole function body before executing it.
Shadows the built-in function for the rest of the scope. Very hard to debug.

keyTakeaways.point: PEP 8: `lowercase_with_underscores` for variables, `UPPER_SNAKE_CASE` for constants, `PascalCase` for classes.
keyTakeaways.why: Convention is not enforced but is expected in every team and code review.

keyTakeaways.point: Closures capture the variable by reference — not its value at creation time.
keyTakeaways.why: The late-binding closure bug: all lambdas in a loop see the loop variable's FINAL value. Fix with default arguments.

keyTakeaways.point: Use `is None` not `== None`. Use `isinstance()` not `type(x) == T`.
keyTakeaways.why: Identity checks are always safe. Equality and type checks can be overridden by custom classes.

keyTakeaways.point: Type annotations are documentation for tools — they are not enforced at runtime.
keyTakeaways.why: You get IDE autocomplete and mypy checking without any runtime cost or restriction.

commonErrors.cause: Variable name starts with a digit: `1score = 95`
commonErrors.fix: Move the digit to the end: `score1 = 95`

commonErrors.cause: Space in variable name: `final score = 100`, or keyword used: `class = 'Math'`, or `=` in condition: `if x = 5:`
commonErrors.fix: Use underscores: `final_score`. Rename keyword clashes. Use `==` for comparison.

commonErrors.cause: Reading a variable before creating it, or typo in the name (case mismatch)
commonErrors.fix: Assign before reading. Check spelling and case.

commonErrors.error: UnboundLocalError: local variable 'x' referenced before assignment
commonErrors.cause: Function has `x = ...` somewhere in its body, so Python treats `x` as local everywhere in the function — including lines before the assignment
commonErrors.fix: Add `global x` at the top of the function, or better: pass `x` as an argument and return the result

commonErrors.error: Unexpected mutation — list changed without direct assignment
commonErrors.cause: `b = a` followed by `b.append(...)` — both names share the same list object
commonErrors.fix: Use `b = a.copy()` or `b = a[:]` to create an independent copy

commonErrors.cause: `list = [1, 2, 3]` shadows the built-in. Later `list([4, 5])` tries to call your variable.

commonErrors.error: All loop-generated functions return the same value
commonErrors.cause: Late-binding closure: `funcs = [lambda: i for i in range(3)]` — all lambdas share one `i`
commonErrors.fix: Use default argument: `funcs = [lambda i=i: i for i in range(3)]`

quickRecap.in30Seconds: A variable is a label in Python. You make one with `name = value`. The label can be moved to any object of any type — Python figures out the type for you. The most important thing to remember: for lists and dicts, `b = a` means both names share the SAME data. Use `.copy()` when you need independence.

analogies.forKids: A variable is like a box with a name written on it. You put something inside. You can open the box and change what's inside. But if you give the box's address to a friend (like you do with lists), they can reach inside and change things too.
analogies.forProgrammers: A variable is an entry in a hash table (the namespace dict). The entry maps a string key (the name) to a PyObject pointer (the reference). Assignment updates the pointer. Mutation changes the object at the pointer's destination.

children: Name bound to an object (not a typed box)
Immutable: int, str, float, bool, tuple
global to assign to module var
nonlocal to assign to enclosing var
UnboundLocalError — assign before read in function
Shared list mutation — b = a for lists
Late-binding closure — lambda captures var, not value

nextTopics.title: Data Types
nextTopics.whyNext: Now that you know what variables ARE, learn what they can HOLD — int, float, str, bool, None — in depth.

nextTopics.title: Type Casting
nextTopics.whyNext: Learn to convert between types: int(), str(), float(). Required for processing user input.

nextTopics.title: Operators
nextTopics.whyNext: Arithmetic (+, -, *, /), comparison (==, !=, <, >), logical (and, or, not). The operations you perform on your variables.

nextTopics.title: String Methods
nextTopics.whyNext: Since strings are immutable and the most common variable type in real applications, their methods (split, join, strip, format) are immediately practical.
